coredata.dc:title: A smart bioresponsive nanosystem with dual-modal imaging for drug visual loading and targeted delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Jingyi
Gong, Peiwei
Li, Shuohan
Kong, Fei
Ge, Xingxing
Wang, Bin
Guo, Lihua
Liu, Zhe
You, Jinmao
coredata.dc:description: Most of present studies are focusing on improving cancer therapy effect by synthesizing new carriers or drugs, while little attention is paid to develop new drug loading patterns and improve the efficiency. Herein, a novel and smart nanosystem that not only realized targeted delivery and responsive degradation but also achieved facilely visual drug loading and release, was reported for the first time. To build this system, highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized and served as an effective indicator and carrier to achieve visual doxorubicin (DOX) loading by the strong fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) interactions between them. Biodegradable nano-MnO2 was then synthesized by biomineralization with folic acid modified bovine serum albumin, which was employed as the other fluorescence quencher for CQDs and DOX and established the other FRET system to visually monitor drug loading. The double-FRET nanosystem could be specifically ingested by cancer cells and responsively degraded by intracellular H+/H2O2 or glutathione. Meanwhile, this triggered drug release behavior was visually detected after gradual dissociation of the two FRET systems by monitoring the fluorescence and magnetic resonance changes. More importantly, both in vitro and in vivo experiments demonstrated that the novel nanosystem exhibited much better cancer therapy effects than pure DOX, and we also reported its first experimental example to treat cancer migration. This work established a new pattern to improve and monitor drug loading, delivery and release, and demonstrated its good performance in cancer therapy and anti-migration.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Structure design
FRET
Drug visual loading
Targeted delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Targeted modification of BSA by folic acid
Drug visual loading and simulated response release tests
Chemical and structural study of MnO2-BSA-FA
Study of CQDs and visual drug loading property
GSH and H+ triggered responsive degradation and drug release
Cell uptake and selectivity toward cancer cells
Intracellular cell uptake and drug release behavior
Intracellular responsive and gradient drug release monitored by CQDs and DOX
In vivo MRI and fluorescence imaging study
In vivo therapeutic effect of MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustrations for construction of this smart double-FRET nanosystem with MRI and PL imaging, and corresponding applications that realize drug visual loading, responsive delivery, and good cancer therapy effect.

(a) TEM image of MnO2-BSA-FA. (b) XRD curves for MnO2 and MnO2-BSA-FA. (c) Energy dispersion spectrum (EDS) of MnO2-BSA-FA. (d) UV-Vis and (e) FTIR analyses of MnO2, BSA, FA, and MnO2-BSA-FA. (f) Dispersibility images for MnO2 and MnO2-BSA-FA in PBS.

(a) TEM image of CQDs. (b) FTIR and (c) UV–Vis data for the samples. (d) Variation of PL emission peaks of CQDs and DOX under different excitation wavelengths. Insets showed corresponding images under the excitation of 365 nm. (e) Fluorescence intensity changes of CQDs after being loaded with different amounts of DOX. (f) The Color of CQDs solution under natural light and ultraviolet light after loading various amounts of DOX.

(a) TEM image of MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX. The inset HR TEM shows the typical lattice of CQDs. (b) Fluorescence intensity changes of DOX with the addition of MnO2-BSA-FA. (c) Color changes of CQDs + DOX solution under natural light and ultraviolet light with the concentration of MnO2-BSA-FA increases from 0 to 1 mg/mL.

(a) Anti-interference degradation study of MnO2-BSA-FA under various ions and amino acids (Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, Ca2Gly, His, Asp, Lys, Val, Asn, Pro, Leu, Hcy, Cys, DTT, GSH). Fluorescence recovery effects of DOX with MnO2-BSA-FA degradation triggered by different amount of (b) GSH and (c) H+/H2O2.
Flow cytometry data obtained by incubating (a) HeLa and A549 cells with MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX. (b) HeLa cells with MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX and MnO2-BSA + CQDs + DOX.
Cellular uptake of MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX for 1 h with DOX concentration of (a) 0.25 μg/mL and (b) 0.5 μg/mL.
Gradient drug release behavior in HeLa cells for (a) 1 h and (b) 4 h under the same conditions (the concentration of DOX and CQDs was 1 μg/mL and 6.75 μg/mL, respectively).

(a) Toxicity of MnO2-BSA-FA and MnO2 toward HeLa cells. (b) Targeting selectivity verification of MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX. (c) Toxicity of different concentrations of pure DOX and MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX toward HeLa cells.

Cell migration activity study. (a) Migration distance ratio of HeLa cells under different experimental conditions. Representative cell migration images: (b) HeLa cells without any treatment. HeLa cells incubated with MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX with DOX concentrations of (c) 0.1 μg/mL and (d) 0.5 μg/mL.
Time-dependent (a) fluorescence imaging and (b) MRI of intratumoral MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX after injection for different hours.

Changes of relative mice (a) body weight (b) tumor volume after different treatments. (c) Representative tumor photographs after being treated for 14 days. (d) Tissue section images of heart, kidney, lung, liver, spleen and tumor after being treated with (i) PBS, (ii) pure DOX, and (iii) MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX.

head.ce:title: A smart bioresponsive nanosystem with dual-modal imaging for drug visual loading and targeted delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis and Key Laboratory of Pharmaceutical Intermediates and Analysis of Natural Medicine, Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis and Key Laboratory of Pharmaceutical Intermediates and Analysis of Natural Medicine
Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis and Key Laboratory of Pharmaceutical Intermediates and Analysis of Natural Medicine, Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Tibetan Medicine Research, Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Xining 810001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Tibetan Medicine Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Tibetan Medicine Research, Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Xining 810001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Plateau Ecology and Agriculture, Qinghai University, Xining 810016, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Plateau Ecology and Agriculture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Plateau Ecology and Agriculture, Qinghai University, Xining 810016, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel and smart bioresponsive nanosystem with dual-modal imaging that realized drug visual loading, targeted delivery and responsive release was constructed and systematically studied, and we also reported its first application in inhibiting cancer migration with much better cancer therapy effects.

Most of present studies are focusing on improving cancer therapy effect by synthesizing new carriers or drugs, while little attention is paid to develop new drug loading patterns and improve the efficiency. Herein, a novel and smart nanosystem that not only realized targeted delivery and responsive degradation but also achieved facilely visual drug loading and release, was reported for the first time. To build this system, highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized and served as an effective indicator and carrier to achieve visual doxorubicin (DOX) loading by the strong fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) interactions between them. Biodegradable nano-MnO2 was then synthesized by biomineralization with folic acid modified bovine serum albumin, which was employed as the other fluorescence quencher for CQDs and DOX and established the other FRET system to visually monitor drug loading. The double-FRET nanosystem could be specifically ingested by cancer cells and responsively degraded by intracellular H+/H2O2 or glutathione. Meanwhile, this triggered drug release behavior was visually detected after gradual dissociation of the two FRET systems by monitoring the fluorescence and magnetic resonance changes. More importantly, both in vitro and in vivo experiments demonstrated that the novel nanosystem exhibited much better cancer therapy effects than pure DOX, and we also reported its first experimental example to treat cancer migration. This work established a new pattern to improve and monitor drug loading, delivery and release, and demonstrated its good performance in cancer therapy and anti-migration.

ce:section.ce:para: Doxorubicin (DOX), a water-soluble broad-spectrum anticancer drug, has obvious chemotherapeutic effect on various cancer cells [1,2]. However, the therapeutic effect is far from satisfactory, which is mostly ascribed to non-specificity toward normal and cancerous cells, diluted concentrations in targeted cells, and cumulative toxicity resulting from blindly increasing drug doses [3,4], etc. One of the most effective methods to avoids these problems is to develop drug delivery systems (DDS) based on nanomaterials, which allows facile surface modification, easy cell uptake, high drug loading contents, and controlled drug release behavior [5]. Thus more and more attention and research interest are paid to construct new DDS, and various nanomaterials including SiO2, carbon nanotubes, graphene, hydrogel, MOFs, and COFs etc. have been employed to delivery DOX [6–11]. However, most of these works are mainly focused on improving the therapeutic effects, while little attention is paid to develop new drug loading patterns and improve the efficiency, especially to detect the drug loading process under naked eyes instead of using employing analytical instruments. Thus it is highly desirable to construct a novel DDS that can not only realize targeted delivery and responsive degradation, but also greatly improve drug loading efficiency and effectively detect the whole loading and release process.

Recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) characteristic of narrow luminescent wave range with high quantum yield, stable emission with long fluorescence life, low toxicity with good dispersibility in many media, high chemical stability with easy synthesis procedures [12,13], are becoming a hot spot in the fields of optical instruments, solar cells, fluorescent sensors, and bioimaging, etc. [14–17]. Especially, the excellent passive targeting effect and long circulation performance further make CQDs a satisfactory nanocarrier for drug delivery, and it has found that due to the aromatic conjugated structure, many anticancer drugs can be easily loaded onto CQDs by π-π or hydrogen-bond interactions [18]. Accordingly, most of the reported works were mainly focused on the synthesis and application of CQDs for bio-imaging and drug delivery, while few studies are carried out to investigate the possibility of CQDs that serves as an indicator to monitor drug loading and release, especially to detect the drug loading behavior under naked eyes instead of using analytical instruments.

On the other hand, nano-MnO2 was widely concerned as a burgeoning drug carrier due to its many natural advantages different from other nanomaterials: (1) Good biocompatibility and facile surface functionalization. The surface of MnO2 can be readily modified by many molecules, and it has been reported that nano-MnO2 possesses negligible toxicity toward cells [19,20], showing great potential as a novel biomaterial. (2) Responsive biodegradability in tumor microenvironment. The acid conditions and relatively high glutathione (GSH) contents in cancer cells can induce positive decomposition of MnO2 into Mn2+ [21,22], the latter is easily excreted out of living bodies by various metabolic pathways, making it a promising biodegradable nanocarrier. (3) High surface area with excellent drug loading capacity. By designing the microstructure, the surface area of MnO2 can be well controlled, which further exhibits strong adsorption of many drugs through van der Waals force [23]. However, with the deepening and development of research, the disadvantages of MnO2 as nanocarriers are also gradually discovered. (1) Poor dispersibility and unsatisfactory long-time stability. The dispersibility of MnO2 can be improved by tuning its sizes into nanoscale or surface modification, however, agglomeration of MnO2 is often a headache for researchers and this often becomes exacerbated with time being prolonged. This greatly limited its further application in drug delivery, which on the contrary requires long-time stability during blood circulation and in human environment. (2) Non-specificity. Recent researches show that carriers with nano-sizes have good passive targeting property resulted from enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect [24,25], while most of reported MnO2 often have a relatively large or small size and finally makes the EPR effect poor. To endow MnO2 with initiative targeting effect, some surface targeted modification processes are developed. However, these methods often required complex reaction procedures, special reagents [26,27], and after modification the drug loading contents often becomes lower. Therefore, it is of great necessary to develop a new and simple synthetic method to endow MnO2 with suitable size, good dispersibility, prolonged stability and specificity toward cancer cells.

To address these challenges, a novel strategy that takes advantage of the outstanding properties of MnO2 and CQDs, and realizes facile synthesis with good specificity and stability, visual detection of drug loading, responsive biodegradation and drug release, as well as effective cancer chemotherapy and anti-migration was demonstrated. To build this multifunctional nanosystem, highly fluorescent N-doped CQDs was first synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method [28], and then anticancer drugs of DOX was loaded. The strong interactions between them facilitated the construction of an effective fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system [29], whose fluorescent property and solution color gradually changed with different contents of DOX being absorbed, finally allowing visual detection of DOX loading process under naked eyes. Meanwhile, well-controlled MnO2 with an average size of 40 nm was first synthesized by a biomineralization method using bovine serum albumin (BSA) [30,31], and to obtain special selectivity toward cancer cells with folic acid (FA) receptors, BSA was pre-modified by folic acid molecule, all of which guaranteed excellent dispersibility, prolonged stability and specificity. CQDs + DOX was then loaded onto MnO2-BSA-FA by van der Waals interactions between them [32], which resulted into the other FRET system, and the fluorescence intensity of CQDs + DOX was gradually decreased with more and more MnO2-BSA-FA being added. This finding allowed us to easily monitor the loading contents of CQDs + DOX just by observing its fluorescence changes, which could also be operated and monitored under naked eyes. After selectively taken into cancer cells and triggered by intracellular GSH or H+/H2O2 conditions, MnO2-BSA-FA responsively decomposed into Mn2+ and this induced first dissociation of the FRET system, resulting into fluorescence recovery of CQDs + DOX. Then DOX was gradually released from CQDs and the other FRET system was dissociated. These fluorescence signal changes and dissociation process could be well observed and employed to monitor the MnO2-BSA-FA decomposition degree, drug release and distribution. These results were systematacially investigated by various chemical, structural, analytical, and cell experiments. Moreover, the anti-migration performance of this novel nanosystem was also evaluated, and both in vitro and in vivo experiments indicated that excellent anticancer effect and anticancer-migration was obtained. An illustration to show this new concept of multifunctional DDS that combines visual drug loading, effective targeted delivery, responsive release, and good chemotherapy and anticancer-migration effects was provided in Fig. 1 .
Chemical reagents for synthesizing MnO2, BSA-FA, MnO2-BSA-FA, and CQDs etc. were given in part 1 of the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Targeted modification of BSA by folic acid
ce:section.ce:para: Targeted modification was carried out in two steps. FA molecular was firstly reacted with n-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) to generate an active ester intermediate (FA-NHS), and then continued to react with BSA to generate BSA-FA. The specific reaction dosage, details and characterization of BSA-FA were shown in Fig. S1 and S2 in supporting information.

MnO2-BSA-FA was synthesized by a biomineralization method. Specifically, 100 mg BSA-FA dissolved in 20 mL deionized water was mixed with 10 mL MnCl2 solution (12.6 mg/mL) and the pH was adjusted to 10 with 1 M NaOH under vigorous stirring. After reacting in dark for 2 h, the Mn2+ would grow into MnO2. The mixed solution was dialyzed by deionized water in the dialysis bag. Finally, the product was obtained by vacuum drying. In order to compare the excellent performance of the new product obtained by biomineralization, pure MnO2 and MnO2-BSA were synthesized by adopting the same method as MnO2-BA-FA. Specific reaction conditions, dosage, products treatment and characterization tests were in part 4 and 5 in supporting information.

CQDs was prepared in one step by a facile hydrothermal procedure with some modifications [28].Various experiments were conducted and the optimal conditions were as follows. 0.27 g hydroquinone was reacted with 3 mL anhydrous ethylenediamine at 180 °C for 12 h to obtain the desired the product (Fig. S3). The specific experimental details were given in supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug visual loading and simulated response release tests
ce:section.ce:para: Both DOX loading on CQDs and CQDs + DOX loading on MnO2-BSA-FA were achieved by physical adsorption process. The mixture was stirred for 24 h in dark to achieve an effective loading. The specific operation steps, dosage and related calculation process for exploring the best loading mass ratio and corresponding relationships between the ratio and fluorescent color in the two loading processes were given in part 7 and 8 in supporting information.

The drug released behavior of MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX was divided into two groups (GSH and H+/H2O2) for study. We studied the possible degradation process in cells through simulating intracellular conditions. These specific processes were in part 9 in supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical and structural study of MnO2-BSA-FA
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2 a, diamond-shaped MnO2-BSA-FA sheets (long axis was around 40 nm and minor axis was around 25 nm) were readily obtained after the facile biomineralization process, and HR TEM images (the inset in Fig. 2a) showed that the average lattice distance was about 0.27 nm, This result is consistent with the reported lattice spacing of nano-MnO2 [33]. Corresponding EDS data in Fig. 2c indicated that obvious elements of C, N, O, Mn and S were detected, suggesting the successful integration of MnO2 and BSA-FA during the biomineralization procedure. And this was then validated by XRD measurement in Fig. 2b. Both pure MnO2 and MnO2-BSA-FA exhibited a broad and characteristic peak centered at 21.36°, indicating poorly crystallized structure of the products [34]. In addition, the synthesis of MnO2-BSA-FA was also be proved by spectral testing. UV–Vis data (Fig. 2d) revealed that pure MnO2 that was synthesized under the same conditions showed a broad absorption peak at about 400 nm. BSA and FA had characteristic absorption peaks at 281 and 365 nm respectively [31,35,36]. These typical peaks were all simultaneously observed on UV–Vis curve of MnO2-BSA-FA, similar results were also revealed by FTIR data in Fig. 2e, in which characteristic peaks of FA (1688 cm−1, 2940 cm−1), MnO2 (526 cm−1, 625 cm−1) and BSA (390 cm−1, 1542 cm−1) [37–39] were detected from MnO2-BSA-FA. All these above data confirmed successful construction of MnO2-BSA-FA, and interestingly, it was found that compared with MnO2, the solubility of MnO2-BSA-FA was significantly improved (Fig. 2f). The solution of MnO2-BSA-FA exhibited good dispersibility even under very high concentrations, and no precipitate appeared even under several months (Fig. S7). This is mostly because that BSA is enriched with functional groups of –NH2 and –COOH [40], and these groups are all hydrophilic ones and thus lead to good water-solubility of MnO2 after modification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of CQDs and visual drug loading property
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare CQDs with high fluorescence and good solubility, a series of experiments were conducted to ascertain the optimal chemical reagents and reaction conditions. TEM test in Fig. 3 a showed that CQDs with uniform sizes were obtained and statistical data in Fig. S4a indicated that the average particle size was about 2~4 nm. FTIR data in Fig. 3b displayed that typical chemical bonds of CC (1513 cm−1, 1620 cm−1), C–N (1340 cm−1), and CO (1225 cm−1) appeared in CQDs [41], and these functional groups further guaranteed good solubility in water and other media. Meanwhile, CQDs contained a large number of benzene rings in its carbon skeleton structure, and DOX was an aromatic compound with benzene rings. So the π-π conjugation between them made it possible for CQDs to load DOX in phosphate buffered solution (PBS) [42]. Moreover, both CQDs and DOX had abundant –OH and –NH2 groups [43,44], and hydrogen bonds easily formed between them to provide interactions for drug adsorption. On the basis of these theories, we tried to load DOX onto CQDs and achieved success. After being stirred together in dark for 24 h and removing unbounded DOX, FTIR was first carried out to investigate the loading effect after lyophilization. It was found that typical bonds of DOX, such as CO (1730 cm−1) and –OH (3300 cm−1, 3550 cm−1)[45], were also detected in the FTIR curve of CQDs + DOX, confirming the successful drug loading. This conclusion was also confirmed by UV data (Fig. 3c). Characteristic peaks of pure CQDs and DOX were located at 405 nm and 495 nm [46,47], respectively. These characteristic peaks were also appeared simultaneously in the UV curve of CQDs + DOX, which further proved the successful loading results. On the other hand, it is worth noting that both CQDs and DOX have excellent photoluminescence (PL) characteristics. They exhibited typical excitation-wavelength dependent PL performance (Figs. 3d and S8) and the optimal excitation wavelengths for CQDs and DOX were 435 nm and 495 nm, respectively. When irradiated by an ultraviolet lamp with 365 nm wavelength, CQDs displayed brightly green emission while DOX showed orange color (insets in Fig. 3d).

Due to the special interactions between them and their unique PL performance, it was speculated that when DOX was loaded onto CQDs, an effective FRET system would be built. Therefore, it was forecasted that with DOX concentration gradually increasing, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs would decrease continuously, and when CQDs were totally covered by DOX, more DOX could change the PL emission curve of CQDs. To verify this assumption, different amounts of DOX was sequentially added into CQDs solution, and the PL investigation showed that with DOX increasing, the PL intensity of CQDs gradually decreased. It’s worth noting that when DOX concentration reached 22.5 μg/mL, the PL intensity reached the lowest value, and any further DOX addition induced the appearance of the characteristic PL emission peak of DOX. Our results indicated that when the amount of DOX reached 20 μg/mL, saturated absorption and drug loading were obtained. This allowed us effectively to detect the drug loading process by PL measurement. More importantly and interestingly, when we exposed those CQDs that absorbed different amounts of DOX with naked eyes under natural or 365 nm UV light, it was observed that the solution color regularly changed with the drug loading contents (Fig. 3f). With DOX concentration increasing, the color of the solution gradually darkened under natural light, and the color basically remained unchanged after the DOX concentration reached 20 μg/mL. The same experimental operation was also recorded under the irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. As showed in the picture, the color of the solution gradually changed from green to yellow-green, and then the color was basically stable, which was consistent with the experimental results obtained by fluorescence investigation. Based on above data and results, it was found that the loading contents of DOX onto CQDs can be facilely and effectively monitored by observing the solution color changes of CQDs with naked eyes. Compared to frequently-used means to monitor drug loading that used spectroscopic instruments like ultraviolet or fluorescence spectrophotometer, our visual loading method can greatly simplify experimental process and improve the efficiency.

Effective loading of CQDs + DOX onto MnO2-BSA-FA was confirmed by TEM image in (Fig. 4 a). It was found that black dots were observed on the surface of MnO2 sheets, and the corresponding HR TEM showed that the lattice spacing of these black dots was 0.31 nm, which was consistent with the lattice spacing of CQDs [48]. Therefore, it could be concluded that CQDs + DOX had been successfully loaded. Moreover, this result was further confirmed by PL investigations. When CQDs + DOX were loaded onto MnO2-BSA-FA, the other effective FRET system was built due to the van der Waals force between them. Therefore, it was also speculated that the PL intensity of CQDs + DOX would gradually decrease with the addition of MnO2-BSA-FA, and total fluorescence quenching could also be obtained when all the CQDs + DOX were absorbed. To investigate this speculation, MnO2-BSA-FA was continuously added to the CQDs + DOX solution, and the fluorescence intensity changes of DOX and CQDs were monitored simultaneously (Figs. 4b and S9). The fluorescence intensity of DOX decreased regularly until total quenching as the concentration of MnO2-BSA-FA increased, the result indicating that when MnO2-BSA-FA concentration reached 429 μg/mL, absorption became saturated and this indicated full loading was achieved.

This important finding then inspires us to study whether it is possible to develop the other method that can monitor the drug loading process of MnO2-BSA-FA with naked eyes. As showed in Fig. 4c, the color of the CQDs + DOX solution gradually deepened from almost colorless to pale yellow to brown under natural light, while the yellow-green color of the solution gradually became lighter under the irradiation of an ultraviolet lamp. Interestingly, further study revealed that when the amount of MnO2-BSA-FA reached 1 mg/mL, it was clear that the yellow-green color of CQDs + DOX almost completely disappeared, suggesting that almost all CQDs + DOX were effectively adsorbed by MnO2-BSA-FA. And this result also realizes visually monitor of drug loading by naked eyes under both natural and UV light, which avoids complex instrumental detection and shows greater simplicity and convenience.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GSH and H+ triggered responsive degradation and drug release
ce:section.ce:para:
Next, we further investigated the controlled and responsive release of MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX system. In order to study the responsive decomposition performance, various substances in human body, including inorganic salt and amino acids, were tested and analyzed (Fig. 5 a). When MnO2 decomposed, the FRET system was destroyed and thus the fluorescence of DOX or CQDs would gradually recover, serving as a probe for monitoring the release of CQDs + DOX from MnO2-BSA-FA. By detecting fluorescence recovery of DOX after adding different ions or amino acids into the fluorescence-quenched solution, the stronger the fluorescence intensity of DOX recovered, the more MnO2-BSA-FA degraded. The results showed that MnO2-BSA-FA was very stable and didn’t react with most of ions and amino acids. It is noteworthy that degradation of MnO2-BSA-FA in the presence of cysteine (Cys) and dithiothreitol (DTT) was observed, and this trend became more obvious in the presence of glutathione (GSH). This is because GSH is reducible and MnO2 is oxidative. Therefore, oxidation-reduction reaction can easily occur. Moreover, the smaller size of MnO2-BSA-FA also makes it easier to be reduced into Mn2+. Therefore, when MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX were taken into cancer cells by endocytosis, it would degrade responsively to the three substances, and thus CQDs and drugs released. To verify this phenomenon, various doses of GSH were sequentially added into the MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX aqueous solution, and then the fluorescence intensity of DOX was measured (Fig. 5b). It was found that the fluorescence of DOX gradually recovered with the increase of GSH. Moreover, since the microenvironment of cancer cells is acidic and contains a large amount of H2O2 [35], it was suggested that MnO2-BSA-FA could also be degraded according to the reaction equation [31].
Mn
O 2
+
H 2
O 2
+ 2
H
+
→ M
n
2 +
+
O 2
+ 2
H 2
O

To verify this, we then measured the fluorescence intensity of DOX after adding various amounts of H+/H2O2 into MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX (Fig. 5c). The results showed that the fluorescence of DOX increased steadily with the increase of H+/H2O2. Astonishingly, the fluorescence of DOX almost completely recovered to the same intensity of pure DOX with same concentrations when 240 μL of H+/H2O2 were added. These results indicated that MnO2-BSA-FA had good responsive sensitivity toward GSH or H+/H2O2, which would greatly reduce the release of drugs in the transport process, decrease the side-effect toward normal cells and improve the therapeutic efficacy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell uptake and selectivity toward cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: Flow cytometry is a powerful means used for studying the cellular uptake behavior, and the uptake of MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX can be evaluated by comparing the intracellular fluorescence intensity of DOX. To study the targeting effects, HeLa cells (positive FA receptors) and A549 cells (negative FA receptors) were chosen, and after being incubated with same concentrations of MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX under the same conditions (Fig. 6 a), it was found that fluorescence intensity of the two cells in control group was nearly the same. However, in the experimental group, the fluorescence intensity of HeLa cells was significantly higher than that of A549 cells. The average fluorescence intensity of HeLa cells was about 48353, while for A549 cells the value was about 21,749 (Fig. S10a), indicating that more MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX were readily taken up by cancer cells with FA receptors and the constructed system had good targeting property. To further study the targeting efficiency, both of MnO2-BSA-FA and MnO2-BSA were prepared under the same conditions. According to the TEM and UV–Vis tests, MnO2-BSA had the same morphological characteristics as MnO2-BSA-FA (Figs. S5 and S6). After loaded by same dose of CQDs + DOX, the two nanocarriers with or without FA were incubated with HeLa cells to study the intakes. Surprisingly, even if the concentration of DOX is very low (0.1 μg/mL), the benefits brought by targeted modification of FA could be clearly observed (Fig. 6b), and the fluorescence intensity enhancement value of MnO2-BSA-FA group was more than two times larger than that of MnO2-BSA group (Fig. S10b). Moreover, in order to study the uptake differences between normal cells and cancer cells, the cell uptake experiments were conducted between HeLa cells and BEAS-2B cells. As shown in Fig. S10c, it was found that after being incubated with MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX under the same concentrations, DOX fluorescence signals in HeLa cells were much stronger than that of normal cells of BEAS-2B, a value of about 5 times stronger was readily obtained (Fig. S10d), indicating MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX were taken more into cancer cells with FA receptors. These experimental data clearly proved that the targeting effect of MnO2-BSA-FA was very significant, which could further greatly increase the selective uptake of drugs by cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular cell uptake and drug release behavior
ce:section.ce:para: The intracellular drug uptake and release behaviors were detailedly studied by laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM, Fig. 7 ). After being treated with MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX for 1 h (Fig. 7a), a weak red fluorescence of DOX mainly appeared in the cytoplasm, while the blue fluorescence of 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI, a classical stain to label the cell nucleus) were found in the nucleus. This revealed that MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX had entered the cell, Even if the concentration of MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX was very small, it can be effectively accumulated in HeLa cells. After being treated with a higher concentration (Fig. 7b), it was found that red fluorescence of DOX in cytoplasm was significantly enhanced, and partly overlapped the blue fluorescence of DAPI. This was ascribed to the reason that with elevated concentration, more MnO2-BSA-FA entered HeLa cells and gradually degraded under GSH or H+/H2O2 conditions, and thus a large amount of CQDs + DOX was further released. Combined with the two sets of data, it is concluded that MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX is easy to be ingested into cells, and the responsive drug release property can be intuitively detected and confirmed by LSCM, which is of great help to enhance the efficacy of DOX delivery and controlled release.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular responsive and gradient drug release monitored by CQDs and DOX
ce:section.ce:para: The intracellular responsive and gradient drug release behavior was studied by LSCM (Fig. 8 and Fig. S11). MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX whose fluorescence was quenched, was added to HeLa cells and cultivated for different times. Fig. 8a showed that after 1 h incubation, green fluorescence signal of CQDs and red fluorescence signal of DOX appeared again in cells. This indicated that MnO2-BSA-FA had degraded after entereing into the cells, and CQDs + DOX was successfully dissociated from the FRET system, all of which finally resulted into DOX release and diffusion into nucleus. With time being prolonged to 4 h, it was found that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs and DOX obviously increased, revealing that MnO2-BSA-FA had been further degraded and a larger amount of CQDs + DOX had been released. It was also worthy noticing that red fluorescence of DOX mainly concentrated in nucleus, while green fluorescence of CQDs only distributed in the cytoplasm. Therefore, responsive decomposition and gradient drug release performance of the novel nanosystem could be monitored by the fluorescence signal changes of CQDs.

As a novel biomaterial, toxicity is the top concern and MTT experiments was first conducted to evaluate toxicity of MnO2-BSA-FA, pure MnO2 and CQDs toward HeLa cells (Fig. 9 a and Fig. S4b). As shown in Fig. 9a, MnO2 without any modification showed some toxicity to cells, and with the concentration increasing, the survival ratio was stable at about 50%. After the biomineralization process, MnO2-BSA-FA greatly improved biocompatibility and the cell survival ratio remained above 80% even under high concentrations. Beside this, it was found that MnO2-BSA-FA also had little cytotoxicity towards BEAS-2B cells (Fig. S12a), which was mostly ascribed to the successful BSA modification. Moreover, the targeting of MnO2-BSA-FA was also verified again by MTT experiment (Fig. 9b). Under the same conditions, MnO2-BSA-FA + DOX exhibited higher cell inhibition toward HeLa cells than that of A549 cells. This good selectivity agreed well with the flow cytometry results in Fig. 6, in which MnO2-BSA-FA + DOX was more readily taken into cells with FA receptors. The excellent performance then urged us to study the cancer therapy effects. Pure DOX, and MnO2-BSA-FA + DOX were incubated with HeLa cells under the same conditions, and obviously MnO2-BSA-FA + DOX showed higher cell growth inhibition than pure DOX at different concentrations (Fig. 9c). This improved therapeutic effect might result from the special selectivity of the nanocarrier toward cancer cells, and sharply reduced intracellular GSH concentration that made the cancer cells become more sensitive towards DOX [49].

The proliferation of cancer cell is the most common cause of cancer patients’ death. Due to mature invasive, cancer cells will obtain multiple gene expressions related to exercise, and often migrate to other tissues after proliferation and division [50]. Therefore, inhibiting the migration of cancer cells during the therapy process is an important way to improve the final therapeutic effect. In vitro scratch method is a simple and economical method to study cell migration [51], and in this work we also used this method. A lot of cell migration activity experiments were conducted and the statistic results were provided in Fig. 10 a. It was found that the migration distance ratio of HeLa cells without any treatment was about 28.6% after free growth for 24 h, but when MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX was added, the cell migration activity was obviously inhibited. And with DOX concentration increase, the inhibition effect became more prominent. When DOX concentration in MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX reached very low concentrations of 0.1 μg/mL and 0.5 μg/mL, the migration distance ratio of HeLa cells was 24.1% and 15.2% respectively. Moreover, it was also worthy noticing that not only the cell migration was heavily inhibited, but also the cell growth state was greatly changed. Specifically, cancer cells without any treatment in Fig. 10b had a good growth state, and it was found that they showed higher transparency and were closely arranged. After being incubated by MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX, HeLa cells exhibited larger intercellular spaces, lower transparency and clearer outlines (Fig. 10c, d). It was reported that cancer cells would send out chemotactic signals before migration, and local actin polymerization would cause cell membrane to form bulges [52]. Therefore, it was speculated suspected that MnO2-BSA-FA could inhibit cell membrane from forming bulge by changing the balance of cell oxidation-reduction state and exerted oxidative pressure on cells, while the released drugs in cell matrix hindered the information exchanges between cells [53]. Therefore, MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX showed novel cancer cell anti-migration property.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo MRI and fluorescence imaging study
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the excellent performance, the in vivo degradation, drug release and distribution behavior of MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX were also studied by fluorescence imaging and MRI. After injection of MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX, the release and distribution of DOX in mice at different time points were monitored (Fig. 11 a). It was found that the fluorescence intensity of DOX was weak just after injection, and its intensity gradually increased with time being prolonged, which was related to the responsive degradation of MnO2-BSA-FA. The DOX content in tumor could be maintained at higher levels with long time, guaranteeing continuous chemotherapy effect. The responsive biodegradation and drug release was further validated by MRI in Fig. 11b. After injection, limited amount of the MnO2-BSA-FA degraded and thus the content of Mn2+ was low, resulting into weak MRI signal [54]. With more and more MnO2-BSA-FA degraded into Mn2+, MRI signal gradually gained intensity and reached maximum after 2 h injection, while gradually lost intensity with more and more Mn2+ being excreted through metabolism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo therapeutic effect of MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX
ce:section.ce:para: The mice were divided into three groups, injected with different samples and bred for two weeks [55,56]. The changes of body weight and tumor volume were recorded continuously, and it was observed that mice treated by MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX continuously gained more weight than that treated by PBS, pure DOX and pure MnO2-BSA-FA (Fig. 12 a and S12b), demonstrating that synergistic therapy didn’t cause obvious toxic effect, and benefitted the normal growth of mice. Meanwhile, Fig. 12b and S12c showed that continuous injection of MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX effectively inhibited tumor growth and reduced its volume, which was much better than pure DOX and pure MnO2-BSA-FA. This further proved that MnO2-BSA-FA had excellent drug targeted delivery ability and tumor accumulation effect, finally resulting into greatly improved therapeutic effect. By studying the histological results from heart, kidney, lung, liver, spleen and tumor tissue of mice (Fig. 12d and S12d), it was found that MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX didn’t cause obvious toxicity and side effects to the organs, holding great potential in developing new concept of drug loading, delivery and cancer therapy patterns.

In summary, a novel and smart nanosystem based on MnO2-BSA-FA and CQDs was constructed. The nanosystem enjoyed many distinguished functions and realized good specificity and stability, visual detection of drug loading, responsive biodegradation and drug release, effective cancer chemotherapy and anti-migration. Effective utilization of biomineralization methods allowed us to synthesize MnO2-BSA-FA with controlled microstructure and designed surface chemistry, which further realized special selectivity toward cancer cells and responsive degradation triggered by intracellular acid or high GSH contents. Rational employment of FRET mechanism enabled us to construct two FERT systems of CQDs + DOX and MnO2-BSA-FA + CQDs + DOX, making it possible to visually monitor the drug loading and release process with naked eyes for the first time, which avoided the use of UV or fluorescence instruments and thus greatly improved the efficiency. Moreover, both in vitro and in vivo experiments revealed that the novel nanocarrier exhibited much better cancer therapeutic effect compared with using pure anticancer drugs under the same conditions, allowing more accurately drug delivery and more concentrated drug release that was effectively responsive to cancer intracellular conditions. And most importantly, the nanosystem also exhibited outstanding anti-migration performance toward cancer cells, showing great potential in future cancer therapy.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by Shandong Province Natural Science Foundation (ZR2018QB002), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51905304, 21671118), Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (J16LA03) of Shandong, and the Taishan Scholars. We also thank the financial support from National Undergraduate Training Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship (201910446027), and thank Dr. Mingqian Wang in Nanjing Normal University for the animal tests and data analyses.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2019.123619.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
